intitule: Lesson 6: Listening Comprehension - Voices on Conservation

enonce.texte:
You will listen to a radio documentary about sustainable waste management in Douala, Cameroon. A local environmental activist, Madame Claire Ngo, is being interviewed. Listen carefully and answer the following questions.

Audio Script (for teacher use):
Interviewer: "Welcome back to 'Green Cameroon.' Today, we're discussing waste management in our largest city, Douala. With me is Madame Claire Ngo from the NGO 'Clean Beach Association.' Madame Ngo, what is the biggest challenge?"

Madame Ngo: "Thank you. The biggest challenge is plastic waste. Every day, tons of plastic bottles and bags end up in the Mfoundi River and then the ocean. This waste blocks drainage systems, causing floods during the rainy season. It also harms marine life.

Interviewer: "What solutions does your association propose?"

Madame Ngo: "We believe in a circular economy. Instead of throwing plastic away, we collect it and have it recycled into paving stones for construction. We have also organized community clean-up campaigns in neighborhoods like Bonamoussadi. Last year, 500 volunteers collected over three tons of waste in one weekend.

Interviewer: "That's impressive. What can an ordinary citizen do?"

Madame Ngo: "Everyone must adopt the '3R' principle: Reduce, Reuse, Recycle. Start by refusing single-use plastic bags. Use reusable water bottles. And separate your waste at home: organic waste, plastics, and glass. If we all do our part, we will have a cleaner, healthier city."
images: A polluted riverbank in an African city with plastic waste and drainage pipes
A group of young African volunteers collecting plastic waste from a beach

questions.texte: Identify the main environmental problem discussed in Douala according to Madame Claire Ngo.
questions.reponse: The main problem is plastic waste (tons of plastic bottles and bags ending up in the Mfoundi River and the ocean).

questions.texte: Explain two consequences of plastic waste mentioned in the documentary.
questions.reponse: Two consequences are: (1) It blocks drainage systems, causing floods during the rainy season. (2) It harms marine life.

questions.texte: Describe the solution proposed by 'Clean Beach Association' for plastic waste.
questions.reponse: The association proposes a circular economy: they collect plastic and have it recycled into paving stones for construction. They also organize community clean-up campaigns.

questions.texte: Justify why the '3R' principle (Reduce, Reuse, Recycle) is important for ordinary citizens.
questions.reponse: The '3R' principle is important because it gives citizens concrete actions to reduce waste at the source. Refusing single-use bags and using reusable bottles directly decreases plastic consumption. Separating waste at home enables effective recycling. This collective effort leads to a cleaner, healthier city and prevents environmental problems like flooding and marine pollution.

intitule: Lesson 6: Grammar in Context - The Causative 'Have' and 'Get'

enonce.texte:
Read the sentences below. Then complete the exercises using the causative structure: have + object + past participle or get + object + past participle.

Example from the documentary: "We collect it and have it recycled into paving stones." (We arrange for someone else to recycle it.)

Structure: Subject + have/get + something + past participle (by someone)

questions.texte: Transform the following sentences using the causative form with 'have' or 'get'.

sousQuestions.texte: Someone collects our household waste every Wednesday. (We ...)
sousQuestions.reponse: We have our household waste collected every Wednesday.

sousQuestions.texte: A technician will install solar panels on the school roof next month. (The school ...)
sousQuestions.reponse: The school will have solar panels installed on its roof next month.

sousQuestions.texte: Madame Ngo asked her assistant to organize a clean-up campaign. (Madame Ngo ...)
sousQuestions.reponse: Madame Ngo got a clean-up campaign organized (by her assistant).

sousQuestions.texte: Someone should empty the public trash bins more often. (We should ...)
sousQuestions.reponse: We should get the public trash bins emptied more often.

questions.texte: Complete the sentences with the correct form of 'have' or 'get' and the verb in parentheses.

sousQuestions.texte: Before the rainy season, we should ______ the drainage system ______ (clean) to prevent floods.
Last year, the association ______ three tons of plastic ______ (collect) during the campaign.
Citizens can ______ their organic waste ______ (transform) into compost for gardening.

intitule: Lesson 6: Reading Comprehension - E-Waste Crisis in Africa

enonce.texte:
Read the article below about electronic waste in Africa.

Africa's Growing E-Waste Problem

Every year, millions of used computers, mobile phones, and televisions from Europe and America arrive in African ports, including Douala and Lagos. Officially, these are donations for 'bridging the digital divide.' However, a recent report by the Global E-waste Statistics Partnership reveals a darker reality. Up to 80% of these imports are non-functional. They are not donations; they are dumping.

This e-waste ends up in informal recycling yards in neighborhoods like Makepe (Douala) or Agbogbloshie (Accra). There, men, women, and sometimes children burn the electronics to extract valuable metals like copper and aluminum. This process releases toxic fumes containing lead, mercury, and arsenic into the air. These chemicals cause severe respiratory diseases, skin infections, and cancer. The soil and water become contaminated, affecting local agriculture and drinking water.

Some African entrepreneurs are fighting back. In Nigeria, the E-Waste Producer Responsibility Organization (EPRO) collects and dismantles electronics safely. In Cameroon, the government recently passed a law requiring importers to pay an eco-tax for recycling. However, enforcement remains a challenge. Environmentalists argue that African nations must reject the role of the world's dumping ground. They call for stricter international regulations and local investment in safe recycling technology.
images: A large pile of discarded computers, phones, and TVs in a landfill in an African city
A worker wearing gloves and a mask dismantling electronic waste at a recycling facility

questions.texte: List two sources of e-waste mentioned in the article.
questions.reponse: Two sources are: used computers and mobile phones (or televisions) from Europe and America.

questions.texte: Explain why most imported electronics are considered 'dumping' rather than 'donations'.
questions.reponse: They are considered dumping because up to 80% of these imports are non-functional (broken). They cannot be used to bridge the digital divide and instead become waste that pollutes the environment.

questions.texte: Summarize the health and environmental consequences of informal e-waste recycling.
questions.reponse: Health consequences include severe respiratory diseases, skin infections, and cancer from toxic fumes (lead, mercury, arsenic). Environmental consequences include contamination of soil and water, which affects agriculture and drinking water.

questions.texte: Evaluate the effectiveness of the Cameroonian government's law requiring an eco-tax on importers.
questions.reponse: The law is a positive first step because it creates a financial mechanism for recycling. However, its effectiveness is limited because enforcement remains a challenge. Without proper monitoring and penalties for non-compliance, importers may avoid paying the tax. The article suggests that more action is needed, including stricter international regulations and local investment in safe recycling technology.

intitule: Lesson 6: Vocabulary - Conservation and Waste Management

enonce.texte: Match the words on the left with their correct definitions on the right.

questions.texte: Match each term with its definition.

sousQuestions.reponse: d. Convert waste into reusable material.
e. Able to be maintained at a certain rate or level without harming the environment.
a. Discarded electronic appliances such as mobile phones, computers, and televisions.
c. Poisonous smoke or gas released from burning materials.
b. An economic system aimed at eliminating waste through the continual use of resources.

questions.texte: Fill in the blanks with the correct word from the box: (waste, conserve, renewable, contamination, campaign)

sousQuestions.texte: We need to ______ water by fixing leaking pipes and using it wisely.
The ______ of the river by industrial chemicals killed many fish.
Solar and wind are examples of ______ energy sources.
The NGO launched a public awareness ______ about plastic pollution.

intitule: Lesson 6: Competence - Designing a School Waste Management Plan

enonce.texte:
Context: Your school in Yaoundé has a serious waste problem. Plastic water sachets, food wrappers, and paper are scattered everywhere. The headmaster has asked your English class to propose a concrete action plan to solve this problem.

Task: Write a formal proposal (250-300 words) addressed to the Headmaster. Use the information from Lesson 6 (3R principle, recycling, clean-up campaigns) to structure your plan. Include a title, an introduction, three specific actions, and a conclusion.
images: A messy school courtyard in Africa with plastic sachets and paper on the ground

questions.texte: Write a formal proposal to the Headmaster for a school waste management plan.
questions.reponse:
**Proposal for a Sustainable Waste Management Plan at Government High School Yaoundé**

**To:** The Headmaster
**From:** English Class, Terminale C
**Date:** [Current Date]
**Subject:** Proposal to address waste management on school grounds

**Introduction**
Following the growing accumulation of plastic waste and litter on our school campus, we propose a three-part action plan based on the 'Reduce, Reuse, Recycle' principle. This plan aims to create a cleaner, healthier, and more environmentally responsible school.

**Proposed Actions**
First, we recommend having four color-coded bins installed across the campus: green for organic waste, blue for plastics, yellow for paper, and black for non-recyclables. We will get these bins painted by the art club. Second, we propose organizing a monthly 'Clean School Hour' every last Friday of the month, where each class will be responsible for collecting waste from a designated zone. Third, we suggest launching a competition between classes for the most waste collected. The winning class will have a tree planted in their name. We can get local businesses to sponsor prizes.

**Conclusion**
Implementing this plan will not only make our school cleaner but also educate students about environmental citizenship. We request your approval to begin with a pilot phase in our class next week. We are confident that if we act now, we will transform our school into a model of sustainability.

intitule: Lesson 6: Speaking - Community Debate on Plastic Bans

enonce.texte:
Situation: The municipal council of your city (Douala or Yaoundé) is considering a law to ban single-use plastic bags completely. Some citizens support the ban, while others oppose it. You will participate in a town hall debate.

Instructions: Prepare arguments FOR or AGAINST the ban. Use the vocabulary and grammar from the lesson (modals: must, should, need to; causative: have/get something done). Then, with a partner, role-play the debate for 3-4 minutes.
images: A town hall meeting in Africa with citizens raising hands to speak

questions.texte: Prepare three arguments FOR the plastic bag ban.
questions.reponse: 1. Plastic bags block drainage systems, causing floods. We must ban them to protect our city. 2. They harm marine life when they end up in the ocean. We should have stricter laws to conserve our environment. 3. Alternatives like cloth bags are reusable and sustainable. Citizens need to adopt better habits.

questions.texte: Prepare three arguments AGAINST the plastic bag ban (from the perspective of a market seller).
questions.reponse: 1. Plastic bags are cheap and convenient for my customers. Alternatives are more expensive. 2. The government hasn't provided affordable alternatives. They should get recycling systems set up first. 3. A ban will cost me customers. Instead, we could have a tax on plastic bags to encourage reuse without banning completely.
